title: Vote on what the best rivalry is in Indiana high school sports
description: We've narrowed down our list to the 25 best rivalries in Indiana high school sports. Vote for which one you think is the best through Sunday, July 26.
fullText:
Last week, we broke down 120 of Indiana's best high school sports rivalries. It was part of a special "America 250" project focusing on the history of Indiana high school basketball by journalists from USA Today Co.

This week, we've narrowed that down to 25 and want you, the readers, to decide which one is the best.

Unlimited voting for this poll will go from Thursday, July 23 to 11:59 p.m. ET Sunday, July 26.

To read about all 120 rivalries, click here.

title: Arizona Senate president who gave 2020 election records to FBI wins attorney general GOP primary
description: Arizona Senate President Warren Petersen (R), who complied with a federal grand jury subpoena and gave the FBI records pertaining to the 2020 elections in March, is projected to win the Republican nom...
fullText:
Arizona Senate President Warren Petersen (R), who complied with a federal grand jury subpoena and gave the FBI records pertaining to the 2020 elections in March, is projected to win the Republican nomination to take on Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes (D) in November, according to Decision Desk HQ.

Petersen, who's served as the top Senate Republican since 2023, defeated Rodney Glassman, a lieutenant colonel in the U.S. Air Force JAG Corps Reserve and an attorney, in the GOP contest for Arizona attorney general.

The Arizona Senate president secured endorsements from Rep. Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.), running for governor, several Republican attorneys general in other states and the Arizona Fraternal Order of Police.

Petersen was involved in the Arizona Senate's independent audit of the 2020 state election results conducted by independent firm Cyber Ninjas, which confirmed former President Biden's victory in Maricopa County that election cycle— a move made as President Trump falsely claimed he won the state and others in 2020.

Mayes ahead of the primary has criticized Petersen for his claims about the 2020 election, saying Petersen "should stop lying about our elections and Republicans running for office must denounce his dangerous rhetoric."

In March, the Arizona Senate president said he turned over 2020 election records to the FBI as the Justice Department investigates unsubstantiated claims of fraud.

During a GOP debate in May, Glassman, a former Democrat-turned-Republican, suggested that officials hadn't done enough to investigate the purported fraud that took place in 2020 but did acknowledge that Biden won the election, according to KTAR.

Multiple audits and investigations have confirmed Biden's victory in 2020.

title: Cincinnati Bengals to induct Bob Trumpy, James Brooks into Ring of Honor
description: Jul. 22—Former Cincinnati Bengals tight end Bob Trumpy and running back James Brooks will be going into the organization's Ring of Honor this year. The Bengals announced Wednesday, July 22 that Trumpy and Brooks were selected as the 2026 Ring of Honor inductees, based on a vote by season ticket holders from a ballot featuring 12 individuals who have played a significant role in the franchise's ...
fullText:
Jul. 22—Former Cincinnati Bengals tight end Bob Trumpy and running back James Brooks will be going into the organization's Ring of Honor this year.

The Bengals announced Wednesday, July 22 that Trumpy and Brooks were selected as the 2026 Ring of Honor inductees, based on a vote by season ticket holders from a ballot featuring 12 individuals who have played a significant role in the franchise's history and tradition.

Trumpy, who played for the Bengals from 1968 through 1977, and Brooks (1984-91) will join Ken Anderson, Willie Anderson, Paul Brown, Isaac Curtis, Corey Dillon, Boomer Esiason, Chad Johnson, Dave Lapham, Tim Krumrie, Anthony Muñoz, Lemar Parrish and Ken Riley in the Bengals Ring of Honor.

The induction ceremony will take place during halftime of the game against the Tennessee Titans on Nov. 1.

Trumpy was a 12th-round draft pick of the Bengals in their inaugural 1968 season and notably scored the team's first-ever receiving touchdown. He went on to play in 128 games with 120 starts over the next 10 seasons and set the standard for future players at his position. Among all tight ends in team history, he has the most receiving yards (4,600), receiving touchdowns (35), yards per reception (15.4) and Pro Bowl selections (four).

"In my opinion, during his time, Bob was the best receiving tight end in the league," Bengals President Mike Brown said in a press release. "He was a top-level player. He had a long stride. He had good hands. Because the safeties couldn't stay with him, he became a productive downfield threat for us. He had something that drove him to be good, and he became a great player."

Brooks remains the franchise's career record holder for average yards per carry (4.8; minimum 500 attempts), while ranking second in team history in rushing yards (6,447) and tied for fifth in rushing touchdowns (37) in his eight seasons. A versatile and complete running back, Brooks also contributed 3,012 career receiving yards to give him 9,459 yards from scrimmage, which is the fourth-most in Bengals history. He also holds three of the team's top 10 highest scrimmage yardage totals in a season, highlighted by a franchise-record 1,773 yards in 1986.

"James was one of the toughest players we ever had," Brown said in the press release. "He was a good runner, and not only was he a good catch out of the backfield, he knew how to get yards after he made the catch. And he was quick. Extremely hard to cover on the goal line. But the thing the numbers don't say that needs being said is he was an exceptional pass protector. It's good he's recognized. He deserves it."

title: Republican Hilton tells Californians they can hate Trump but still vote for him in governor's race
description: Republican Steve Hilton launched his general election campaign for California governor Wednesday with a gambit to win over left-leaning voters who dominate the state: Even if they loathe President Don...
fullText:
LOS ANGELES (AP) — Republican Steve Hilton launched his general election campaign for California governor Wednesday with a gambit to win over left-leaning voters who dominate the state: Even if they loathe President Donald Trump, he said, they should consider voting for him.

During an event in Los Angeles, against the backdrop of a stinking, scorched food warehouse he described as a symbol of the state's political incompetence, Hilton rolled out a list of campaign promises clearly aimed at residents buckling under California's notoriously high cost of living. He vowed to bring the price of gasoline down to $3 a gallon, about half the current cost, cut residents' utility bills in half, now among the nation's highest, and eliminate state income taxes on the first $150,000 in earnings.

He blamed the state's problems — from homelessness to low student test scores — squarely on Democrats, who hold every statewide office and supermajorities in the Legislature. He called Democrats a "rotten regime that has abandoned the working class."

In his campaign remarks and ads he took out in leading newspapers throughout the state, Hilton addressed his relationship with Trump, who has endorsed him in the race.

"This is not about who you hate in Washington. It's about what we love about California and what we can do to make California once again the best place anywhere in the country to start and raise a family, to start and run a business," he said before a small group of supporters. "That's what this election is about."

Hilton's maneuver is meant to create a sliver of distance from Trump without breaking from him. It's a tacit nod to the political math in California, one of the nation's most solidly Democratic states. Registered Republicans are outnumbered nearly 2-to-1 by Democratic voters, and a GOP candidate hasn't won a statewide election since 2006.

Trump is widely unpopular in California outside his loyal conservative base. In 2016, Trump lost to Democrat Hillary Clinton by 30 points; in 2020, he lost to Joe Biden by 29 points; and in 2024, he lost to Kamala Harris by 20 points.

California is regarded as the home of the so-called Trump resistance, where Democrats have effectively used Trump as a foil in campaigns to motivate voters. Last year, Gov. Gavin Newsom essentially nationalized the state's fight over Proposition 50, which recast U.S. House districts to favor Democrats, by framing it as a confrontation with Trump. The measure notched a 29-point win.

The campaign of Hilton's Democratic opponent, former U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Xavier Becerra, scoffed at the notion of Hilton being a change agent.

"Californians already know who Steve Hilton is: Trump's puppet in California. His campaign is a promise to run our state the way Trump runs the country and we're not buying it," campaign spokesman Jonathan Underland said in a statement.

As for Hilton's pitch that voters who dislike Trump should feel free to consider him in November, Underland said, "Steve Hilton cheers on a president whose illegal war has sent gas prices soaring and illegal tariffs have cost California families more than $2,500 this year alone. You can't fix the affordability crisis while carrying water for the man causing it."

In a heavily Democratic state, Hilton's campaign is hoping his appeal to Democrats and independents will "give voters a permission slip" to look past the letter next to a candidate's name, even in a nation deeply divided by partisanship.

"You do have a choice if you think that this state is going in the wrong direction," he told supporters at a kickoff event in Boyle Heights, a Los Angeles neighborhood where residents have been complaining about the stench of rotting food, flies and rodents that followed a fire last month at a cold storage warehouse.

He was accompanied by Gloria Romero, a former Democratic state lawmaker who switched parties in 2024 and is this year's Republican nominee for lieutenant governor. She delivered her own address in both English and Spanish, with supporters holding "Hilton-Romero" signs in the background.

Becerra, a former state lawmaker, congressman and state attorney general, is heavily favored to win the seat now held by Newsom, a likely 2028 Democratic presidential candidate who is barred by law from seeking a third term. Hilton and Becerra emerged from a chaotic primary election in June that had more than 50 candidates on the ballot.

The race is unfolding at a time when California is facing multiple crises. Despite billions in spending, homelessness remains widespread, especially in larger cities. Multibillion-dollar budget gaps are projected into the future. Home prices remain out of reach for many residents. The home insurance market is in disarray, businesses and residents have been fleeing the state, and residents contend with some of the nation's highest gas prices, taxes and utility bills.

Making good on his campaign promises won't be an easy task for Hilton, a former adviser to ex-United Kingdom Prime Minister David Cameron who has never held elective office. The governor doesn't control the oil or utility markets, but Hilton says he will get rid of mandates, taxes and fees that drive those prices higher. He also would likely need the consent of a Legislature controlled by Democrats to change the state's income tax structure.

Hilton crowned his campaign kickoff with a 30-second TV ad underscoring the financial challenges for many families.

"Life here has become impossible for anyone who isn't already rich," he says. "Let's fix California."

title: Fetterman calls out Trump over 2020 election claims: 'You're the president'
description: U.S. Sen. John Fetterman criticized President Donald Trump after Trump's comments about alleged election interference during a primetime address.
fullText:
Following President Donald Trump's remarks during a primetime address to the nation accusing China of election interference, U.S. Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pennsylvania, lambasted the president for his focus on unsubstantiated claims of election fraud.

Here's what Fetterman said as he rebuked Trump's recent election fraud claims.

Fetterman chided Trump for his continued insistence that extensive voter fraud swung the 2020 presidential election.

"I don't know why the president is dragging up all of this stuff," Fetterman said on CNN's July 19 "State of the Union." "You're the president. Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020."

Fetterman's criticism comes after Trump flip-flopped on criticisms toward China, initially alleging that the country had interfered in the 2018 midterms and the 2020 presidential election, but later saying he believed China had changed.

Fetterman further called out the president's fixation on 2020 election fraud during his appearance on "State of the Union."

"That's been settled over six years ago," Fetterman said. "It's deeply unhelpful, and that distracts important things, especially like the Iranian war right now."

During the discussion, Fetterman emphasized that Pennsylvania, where Fetterman was serving as lieutenant governor in 2020, elected former President Joe Biden and that the election was "absolutely secure, fair and accurate."

"That was the voice of Pennsylvania, and they picked Joe Biden in 2020," Fetterman said. "That's a fact."

Fetterman also said if he saw evidence that more than 250,000 migrants were registered to vote, "that would make me reconsider some of these things."

"But there's no evidence, and I don't expect that we will see any of that," Fetterman said. "And I also would remind people, in Pennsylvania, out of 67 counties, (the) vast, vast, vast majority of them are deep red counties, and they have never encountered any kinds of fraud."

For nearly six years, Trump has repeatedly claimed election fraud took place during the 2020 election when Biden won despite the Department of Justice finding no evidence of widespread voter fraud.

Most recently, Trump called the election system "broken and so vulnerable that no one can possibly defend it" during a primetime address to the nation on July 16. Referencing declassified intelligence documents, he also alleged that China had attempted to influence the results of the 2018 midterm elections and had tried to stop him from getting elected in 2020, when, he said, the country compromised voter data and stole 220 million voter files from states across the U.S. The effort, Trump claimed, was carried out by Beijing to favor Biden in "the largest compromise of election data in history."

"The cover-up of this colossal security breach is even more disturbing in light of the additional information showing that China engaged in other election-related activities to undermine my first administration and our 2020 campaign," Trump said. "They did not want, they just didn't want it, (and) they fought like hell not to have it, Donald Trump to win, and for good reason."

But just days later, Trump downplayed China's alleged actions, saying he believed the country has changed since Biden's election and that the U.S. also targets China, though he wasn't specific about what U.S. actions he was referencing.

"I think China's maybe a little bit different today than it was then," Trump said. "But look, they do things. And we do things to them. I'll be honest. We do things to them, too. It's not a one-way street. But we'll be talking to them."

Finch Walker is the Pittsburgh Connect Reporter for the USA TODAY Network. Contact Walker at FWalker@usatodayco.com. Instagram: @finchwalker_. X: @_finchwalker.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Fetterman calls out Trump over 2020 election claims: 'You're the president'

title: Vote for the SouthCoast's top golf course in 2026
description: A dozen of the area's local golf courses are in the running for the SouthCoast's top golf course in 2026. Voting ends on Friday, July 31.
fullText:
With the summer in full swing, now is the perfect time to take a look at some of the best golf courses on the SouthCoast.

From private clubs to public courses, there are several options for local golfers. Modern ones (m) were built in 1960 or later while classic ones (c) were built before 1960.

Here are some of Greater New Bedford's best golf spots, listed alphabetically by name.

Here’s your chance to vote for your favorite with our SouthCoast Golf Course of the Year for 2026 poll.

The 18-hole course, located at 685 Main St. in Acushnet, offers four sets of tees measuring 5,099 to 6,807 yards, allowing golfers of all skill levels to enjoy their rounds of golf.

The 18-hole, par-72 championship layout offers an exceptional golf experience for new players and established golfers. The course is located at 1047 Allen St. in Dartmouth.

The members-only country club, located at 585 Slocum Rd. in North Dartmouth, features an 18-hole course known for its storied tradition as the home of the CCNB Men’s Four-Ball, which is in its 96th year.

Located at 17 Heritage Hill Dr. in Lakeville, the Heritage Hill course has a two-in-one layout; built to play as a par 54 or par 60 and is geared toward seasoned golfers as well as junior golfers.

The 18-hole, par 72 public golf course, which is known for its three picturesque fishing holes, is located at 44 Clear Pond Rd. in Lakeville.

Located at 1 Little Harbor Rd. in Wareham, Little Harbor is a well-conditioned 18-hole, par 56 golf course open to the public.

Poquoy Brook, which is located at 20 Leonard St. in Lakeville, is a public 18-hole, parkland-style golf course built in the 1960s.

Established in 1904, Marion Golf Club is a public 9-hole course located at 10 South Dr. in Marion.

Located at 10 Reservation Rd. in Mattapoisett, Reservation Golf and Beach Club, which was established in 1895, is a private course and club only open to its members and their guests. The course has amazing views of Mattapoisett Harbor, Eel Pond and the Mattapoisett River.

Nestled inside pristine woods, the Rochester Golf Club is a regulation 18-hole public golf course located at 323 Rounesville Rd. in Rochester.

The Bay Club features a private championship 18-hole course designed by PGA Tour professional Brad Faxon and golf course architect Brad Booth. It is located at 63 County Rd. in Mattapoisett.

An 18-hole public golf course and driving range located at 581 Hathaway Road in New Bedford.

This article originally appeared on Standard-Times: Vote for the SouthCoast's top golf course in 2026

title: 6,600 noncitizens registered to vote after error in New Jersey motor vehicle system, governor says
description: About 6,600 people who indicated they were noncitizens while obtaining New Jersey driver's licenses and other state IDs were improperly registered to vote in 2023 and 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said Tu...
fullText:
HADDONFIELD, New Jersey (AP) — About 6,600 people who indicated they were noncitizens while obtaining New Jersey driver's licenses and other state IDs were improperly registered to vote in 2023 and 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said Tuesday.

The Democratic governor, who took office this year, blamed a Motor Vehicle Commission system software error but said a preliminary analysis showed that fewer than 400 of those who registered that way went on to cast ballots.

The revelation comes as President Donald Trump and other Republicans continue to assert that voting by noncitizens is rampant in U.S. elections, even though it's rare and, when caught, can be punished as a felony that can lead to deportation.

Sherrill promised immediate action and said she has ordered that all erroneously added voter registrations from that period be removed. She also is replacing the vendor who ran the system and is launching an investigation into how the error happened.

"I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time," she said in a statement.

She also attempted to head off any future criticism of the issue from the president.

"As the Trump Administration tries to weaponize elections for political gain, I am ensuring we protect our elections," she said.

The White House on Tuesday used the revelation to promote Trump's SAVE America Act, which would require people to show documentary proof of citizenship before they register to vote and impose voter ID requirements nationwide. The measure has passed the U.S. House but has not gotten a vote in the Senate, where even some Republicans oppose it.

"American voters deserve to have confidence that our elections are safe and secure," White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement.

Sherrill also noted that the noncitizens who registered did so as Democrats, Republicans and independents. About 6.9 million people in New Jersey are registered to vote and more than 4 million cast ballots in the 2024 presidential election.

Phil Murphy, also a Democrat, was governor when the error occurred. A representative for the former governor did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Christine Hanlon, chair of the Republican party organization in New Jersey, said voter registration needs to be improved and that a bipartisan investigation should dig into what happened, including why the registrations weren't disclosed previously.

"The system and verification process must be improved in order to safeguard our elections," she said in a statement Tuesday. "The public also deserves to know why this was covered up and who was responsible for keeping this critical information from the public."

In a speech last week, Trump touted an investigation from the Department of Homeland Security, which said it had identified about 278,000 noncitizens registered to vote in federal elections across the country. It said most of them were in California, Nevada, New Jersey and Pennsylvania.

Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin on Tuesday suggested it was that report that prompted Sherrill to take action.

"Appreciate you responding back to our letter, @GovSherrillNJ. This should've been done a LONG time ago," he said in a message on X. "Just ONE illegal vote cancels out the vote of a U.S. citizen."

The Pennsylvania Department of State said in an emailed statement provided by Gov. Josh Shapiro's office Tuesday that "evidence has shown that noncitizen voting is extremely rare across the country, including in Pennsylvania." The department added that it would review any information the Department of Homeland Security provided to "evaluate the validity of these claims."

New Jersey is among 19 states that allow people who are in the U.S. illegally to hold driver's licenses and other state-issued ID cards, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. Every state allows noncitizens who have legal status in the U.S. to hold a license.

The NCSL also says that about half the states have automatic voter registration in which eligible people who obtain licenses or, in some cases, other state services, can opt in to voter registration.

In Oregon, officials in 2024 found that about 1,600 people were registered to vote through the state's DMV even though they didn't show proof of citizenship. Most of them were later confirmed to be citizens. The number of people wrongly registered grew by more than 100 by last year, but the state found that only about 30 ineligible voters had cast ballots.

Colorado officials said in 2022 that they had mistakenly sent postcards to 30,000 noncitizens to encourage them to register. The state said none would be allowed if they tried to do so.

A 2019 state audit of California's then-new DMV registration program found numerous errors, some that might have allowed about 1,500 people to incorrectly register to vote. Of those, some might not have been U.S. citizens.

title: Reporter's Notebook: The Republican-led vote that revealed Democrats' new divide on Israel
description: A House vote to cut $3 billion in aid to Israel split Democrats nearly down the middle, with 103 voting to slash assistance to the Israeli military.
fullText:
Things sometimes evolve slowly in politics. There's a perception, axiom or maxim which is agreed upon in politics. But over time, it changes.

Sometimes the change is subterranean. The tectonic political plates move. But no one is quite aware of what's happening until one event crystallizes the shift.

Such is the case with support in the Democratic Party for Israel.

House Democrats Fracture Badly Over Massie Amendment To Cut $3.3B In U.s. Aid To Israel

The politics of supporting Israel have moved for several years now. Especially after Israel's response to the deadly attacks by Hamas and pro-Palestinian groups on October 7, 2023.

But then there's that one moment which seems to change everything.

That was certainly on display in the House of Representatives during a single roll call vote last week.

Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., engineered an amendment to a State Department funding bill to cut off $3 billion in aid to Israel. Some Democrats viewed this as a political effort to cleave the party.

Whether it was political or not remains to be seen. But Massie's amendment split Democrats nearly down the middle.

The House defeated Massie's effort, 314 to 104. Massie was the only Republican who voted in favor of slashing aid to Israel. But 103 Democrats joined Massie to also suspend the assistance. Ten Democrats voted "present," only 98 Democrats voted to reject Massie's effort.

The Democratic divide over Israel has widened for a while now. It used to be rare to have more than a few Democrats who wouldn't stand beside Israel. But that House roll call vote truly underscored where the party is now. It exposed deepening internal fissures.

"This is an important moment because nothing will be the same on this issue ever again," observed Rep. Greg Casar, D-Texas, chair of the House Progressive Caucus. "It used to be that just a small number of House Democrats would vote against sending taxpayer dollars and weapons for the Israeli military. Then, two years ago, 37 House Democrats voted to not send billions of dollars to the Israeli military."

Casar added that this was "not the end. This is a new beginning" for Democrats.

"Democrats here in the House have finally decided to do what the people, our voters, the constituents we represent, have been asking us to do," said Rep. Ilhan Omar, D-Minn. "I am proud that they have finally decided to lead with their morals."

The left says the party is simply reflecting where its core voters stand.

New York Times Raises Eyebrows By Referring To Aipac As 'Hard-right' Pro-israel Group

"You're seeing a shift. The American people are growing weary of this war and what's happening," said Rep. Julie Johnson, D-Texas.

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., argued the vote wasn't "a litmus test" for the party. But said the vote addressed another phenomena.

"Americans of all backgrounds are deeply frustrated that our needs are not being met at home. But our resources are being sent abroad in a way that is not representative of our values and not representative of who we are," said Ocasio-Cortez.

Former House Majority Leader Steny Hoyer, D-Md., is retiring from his seat early next year. Few lawmakers from either party have been as vocal as Hoyer in supporting Israel over the past four decades.

"This amendment would embolden the enemies of peace, those pursuing the complete elimination of Israel and those who seek the death of Jews," said Hoyer. "It's a vote against American security."

Hoyer stepped back from the House Democratic leadership in early 2023. But today's Democratic leaders concede the party currently views Israel through a different lens.

"It's not lost on me that the actions the (Israeli) Prime Minister has taken has not made it easier for us to be supporters of a strong U.S.-Israel relationship," said House Democratic Caucus Chairman Pete Aguilar, D-CA.

Even Democrats who voted in favor of the aid say there are shifts in Israel which prompted Democrats to recalibrate their views.

"I think a lot of people are concerned about Netanyahu and are concerned about some of the activities over the past couple of years," said Rep. Tom Suozzi, D-N.Y.

Rep. Wesley Bell, D-Mo., voted against cutting off money for Israel. Bell is a freshman who defeated progressive former Rep. Cori Bush, D-Mo., in the primary two years ago. He then won the general election that fall. Bush is a skeptic of Israel. She is challenging Bell in next month's primary.

"It's important that we do stand with our allies because our sworn enemies are not going away," said Bell.

It's unclear if Bell's vote – pitted against Bush's more progressive stance – could dictate who wins the primary. But it's worth watching.

Pro-Israel Republicans worry about the anti-Israel tide shift on the Democratic side of the aisle.

"I think they've been captured by crazies. You can see it in their elections by people who hate Jews." Said Rep. Randy Fine, R-Fla.

But the new positioning by the Democratic party concerns Sen. John Fetterman, D-Penn., He's a staunch ally of Isreal. Fetterman is threatening to desert Democrats if the party deserts Israel.

"That's my red line in the Democratic party," said Fetterman. "Look at the people that are winning these primaries right now. They're not just 'I'm just pro-Palestinian. I am deeply anti-anti-Israel.'"

Sen John Fetterman Pledges To Be 'Last Man Standing' In The Democratic Party In Support Of Israel

Notably Fetterman did not say that he would necessarily become a Republican. But Democrats need Fetterman in their fold.

Republicans hold a 53-45 advantage over the Democrats. Sens. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., and Angus King, I-Maine, caucus with the Democrats. That gets you to 47 senators who align with the Democrats. So Democrats need a net win of four seats to seize control of the Senate next year. Democrats hope to hold Michigan, Georgia and New Hampshire. But they are also looking to flip seats in Maine, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, Iowa and Alaska. Any combination of four seats scores Democrats the majority.

But let's say Democrats do collect four seats and earn an effective 51-49 advantage. Fetterman could make the Senate 50-50 if he bolts from the Democrats. The Senate has historically worked out a power-sharing arrangement during other 50-50 breakdowns. But regardless, Republicans would have nominal control thanks to the tie-breaking vote of Vice President JD Vance, who is President of the Senate.

The Democrats need Fetterman more than he needs them right now.

And as for Israel? Israel needs Democrats more than they need Israel.

Congressional Republicans still support Israel for now. But if Democrats win control of the House - and perhaps the Senate - support for Israel will look very different next year thanks to the Democrats' morphing politics.

That's all evolved for some time now. But you can point to last week's vote as the seminal moment when it all changed for the Democratic party.

Original article

title: Trump downplays Chinese election interference five days after describing breach of historic proportions
description: Five days after blaming China for “the largest compromise of election data in history,” President Donald Trump shrugged off Beijing’s actions and said the United States was doing the same thing.
fullText:
Five days after blaming China for "the largest compromise of election data in history," President Donald Trump shrugged off Beijing's actions and said the United States was doing the same thing.

Trump said vaguely on Tuesday that the US would discuss the matter with Chinese officials. But he previewed no punishment and claimed the alleged breach was in the rearview mirror.

"It took place a long time ago. I think China is maybe a little bit different today than it was then," he said, despite his accusation last week during a primetime speech that China had illicitly acquired 220 million US voter files starting in 2020 and that the "data loss presents an unprecedented election security nightmare."

Trump on Tuesday then said the alleged infringement was no different from US actions against China.

"They do things and we do things to them," he said. "I'll be honest, we do things to them too. It's not a one-way street."

A day after Trump's speech, a White House official said planning was continuing apace for President Xi Jinping's state visit to the United States in September.

The White House did not respond when asked whether Trump was considering any repercussions for China, including sanctions.

title: Election voting advice from AI chatbots ‘inaccurate and unreliable’
description:
Research during Hungary election shows AI recommended parties not running and gave highly volatile answers to identical prompts
AI chatbots provide inaccurate, inconsistent and unreliable guidance to voters asking which party they should back, a study suggests, often recommending the wrong party, not mentioning the right one, or listing parties not even running.
“The results raise serious concerns about the reliability of general-purpose AI systems in electoral contexts,” the study, published by the civil liberties group Liberties and based on research during this year’s Hungarian parliamentary elections, concluded.

fullText:
Research during Hungary election shows AI recommended parties not running and gave highly volatile answers to identical prompts

AI chatbots provide inaccurate, inconsistent and unreliable guidance to voters asking which party they should back, a study suggests, often recommending the wrong party, not mentioning the right one, or listing parties not even running.

“The results raise serious concerns about the reliability of general-purpose AI systems in electoral contexts,” the study, published by the civil liberties group Liberties and based on research during this year’s Hungarian parliamentary elections, concluded.

“They misclassified profiles, omitted relevant parties and included parties not running in the election,” said the report, shared with the Guardian. Outcomes were also highly volatile, with “materially different” answers given to identical prompts.

The report’s most striking finding from the Hungarian election – convincingly won by Péter Magyar’s opposition Tisza party – was an overwhelming bias in party visibility, particularly as regards Tisza: in 90% of cases when ChatGPT was fed a detailed Tisza-aligned voter profile, it failed to recommend the party.

Magyar’s landslide brought to an end Viktor Orbán’s 16-year grip on power as prime minister and head of his national-conservative Fidesz party.

The researchers noted that unlike voting advice sites or independent media explainers, general-purpose AI (GPAI) systems did not disclose how they generated political guidance, did not yield reproducible results, and were not subject to election-related public oversight.

However, their responses often look confident and authoritative, making users more likely to trust them. With 29.8% of Hungary’s population identified as AI users, the researchers tested the advice given by the two most popular, ChatGPT and Gemini.

Based on party positions outlined on Voksmonitor, a respected Hungarian voting advice app, Liberties created five distinct voter profiles, each aligned with one of the five parties registered on national lists for Hungary’s parliamentary elections.

Each voter profile was then tested 10 times in ChatGPT and 10 times in Gemini, using two different requests: one for direct advice on which party the fictitious voter should back, and another for a percentage match with each of the five parties.

In percentage-matching tests, ChatGPT assigned Tisza a score in just 2% of cases. Instead, users with Tisza-aligned views were routinely recommended a smaller party unlikely to cross the 5% threshold for parliament, or parties not on the national ballot.

The analysis does not claim that the outcome of the election was affected: Tisza still won decisively. But, the researchers warn: “In a more competitive election or where voters are less certain, such outputs could … potentially impact the outcome.”

Other issues included inconsistency, with the same voter profile matched with radically different parties in successive tests, and the inclusion – in 96% of responses from ChatGPT and Gemini – of parties not on the 2026 ballot.

The researchers also noted that both AI models regularly began their responses with polite disclaimers saying they “cannot give political advice” – before providing several paragraphs of dense, highly persuasive party recommendations.

“The answers appeared well-argued, precise and authoritative,” the researchers said. “This creates a risk that users may treat the outputs as reliable, even though the underlying method is opaque and the results are unstable.”

The report argues that the underlying cause of the errors probably lies in training data gaps, filters and language-processing limitations. Tisza surged to prominence only after 2024, meaning static AI training models struggled to place it on their map.

But they said the findings exposed a serious regulatory gap: the EU’s AI Act obliges providers of GPAI models to assess systemic risks, and its Digital Services Act covers “systemic risks” to electoral processes – but AI chatbots fall between the two.

Liberties said safeguards should be developed for AI systems providing political advice, and AI providers should stop offering personalised voting recommendations unless they could guarantee transparency, accuracy, consistency and accountability.

“General-purpose AIs should not present opaque and unstable political matching as if it were reliable electoral guidance” for voters, said Eva Simon, the head of Liberties’s tech and rights programme.

“Democracy cannot rely on opaque systems that claim neutrality while delivering advice they cannot explain, reproduce or guarantee to be accurate.”

title: Why Newcastle are changing club badge
description: Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest as the club look to change their badge for the first time since 1988.
fullText:
Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest, with the club poised to change their badge for the first time since 1988.

The existing emblem is popular with supporters, but Newcastle feel it "struggles to perform in a modern setting".

"Its intricacies and irregularities make consistent, high-quality reproduction impossible - from modern digital platforms to broadcast environments and merchandise," the club said.

Newcastle's plans to update the crest were first publicly mooted in May 2025.

The club have discussed "potential routes forward" with the fan advisory board and surveyed 19,500 members and season ticket holders.

Supporters' feedback helped shape the options, which will ultimately be presented on the ballot.

A total of 84% of those surveyed made it clear they wanted Newcastle to "honour our heritage" rather than completely overhaul the crest.

Supporters also ranked which elements of the current crest they believed were most important, including the castle, seahorses, black and white shield and ribbon.

Eligible voters - a group that comprises season ticket holders, members and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's home matches last season - will pick their favoured design during a two-week window in August.

BBC Sport asked supporters for their views and one respondent, Ian, suggested voters should have a "none of the above option".

"It's hard to say supporters are getting their say when the club came out and said this is happening whether anyone likes it or not," added another, Matt.

"These new crests represent a simplified, homogenised version of everything that has ramped up post-Covid.

"I can already see the final product; a stripped-down version of a crest that is more caricature than representative of anything meaningful."

However, Newcastle supporter Phil highlighted how the club "at least seem to be listening to fans instead of barrelling into some soulless, corporate design".

Another supporter, Andy, "loves" the existing badge, but also stressed "we have to understand that things change".

"Whatever brand you look at, there has been evolution to meet the demands of the modern world," he added.

"We should be no different. It seems like they're trying to go about it in the right, respectful way, but we have to let it happen."

Gifted, charismatic and passionate amid highs and lows - Keegan obituary

How have missed targets affected Newcastle's summer rebuild?

title: Former Portugal coach explains why Bernardo Silva will thrive at Real Madrid: ‘They have signed a phenomenal player’
description: Real Madrid’s decision to sign Bernardo Silva has received a major vote of confidence from one of the people who knows him better than most. Despite the midfielder enduring a difficult 2026 FIFA Wo...
fullText:
Real Madrid’s decision to sign Bernardo Silva has received a major vote of confidence from one of the people who knows him better than most.

Despite the midfielder enduring a difficult 2026 FIFA World Cup with Portugal, former Portugal manager Fernando Santos believes the 31-year-old remains one of the world’s finest footballers and expects him to flourish under Jose Mourinho.

Bernardo Silva’s move to Real Madrid has sparked plenty of debate following his arrival at the Santiago Bernabeu.

While some questioned whether the veteran midfielder is still capable of performing at the highest level, Santos has dismissed those doubts and insists Real Madrid have signed one of the best midfielders in world football.

Having worked closely with Bernardo Silva for years at international level, Santos believes Real Madrid have secured far more than just a technically gifted footballer.

“As a footballer, everyone already knows him; he has immense quality and sees the game in a way that very few players in the world do.

“His standards as a player are so high that it’s hard to say he’s on the same level as a person, but in his case it’s true. He’s considerate, polite, friendly and always has a smile on his face.

“On a personal and human level, he’s one of the finest footballers I’ve ever met in my entire career. Real Madrid have signed a phenomenal player in every sense of the word,” Santos told AS.

Despite turning 31, Santos believes the Bernardo Silva Real Madrid transfer has come at exactly the right time.

“From what I’ve seen of him this season at City, I’d say so. I know him inside out, and I’m convinced he still has some very good years ahead of him.

“He’s still at the peak of his career and he’s going to prove it. For me, he remains one of the best in the world.

Bernardo Silva had an underwhelming World Cup campaign. (Photo by Dan Mullan/Getty Images)

“I think Real Madrid have made the right move in signing him and he’s going to contribute a great deal to their team.”

One of the biggest talking points following the Real Madrid transfer has been how Bernardo Silva will fit into a squad that already features Kylian Mbappe, Vinicius Jr. and Jude Bellingham.

Santos believes that will never be an issue.

“Great players know how to adapt to every situation and circumstance in a match.

“He’s very good at that and won’t have any trouble playing wherever he’s asked to. I’m sure Mourinho has a specific role in mind for him, and Bernardo will fulfil it perfectly,” he explained.

Competition for places is expected to be fierce at the Bernabeu, but Santos believes Mourinho signed the Portuguese international because he sees him playing a major role.

“He’s used to competing, and his quality will see him earn his place. I think he’s destined to play a very important role; that’s why Mourinho has signed him.

“Perhaps he might play closer to the right flank and drift into the centre from time to time, but wherever he’s deployed, he’ll do brilliantly thanks to his tactical intelligence and his reading of the game.

“Mourinho is shrewd, highly skilled in strategic concepts, and he’ll bring out the best in him.”

Santos also rejected suggestions that the pressure of playing for Real Madrid could affect the midfielder, especially after two disappointing seasons for the club.

Fernando Santos has backed Bernardo Silva. (Photo by Leonardo Fernandez/Getty Images)

When asked whether Bernardo Silva shares similarities with Luka Modric, he preferred not to make direct comparisons.

“I don’t really like comparing footballers because each one has their own characteristics, but it’s clear that they share similarities in their style of play.

“I’d say the two main ones, which they both have in common, are their immense technical ability and their commitment to the team.”

He also expressed complete confidence that the Portuguese star will embrace the challenge of playing at the Santiago Bernabéu.

“That won’t be a problem for him at all. He has plenty of experience, he’s played for some of the biggest clubs and he’ll know how to cope with the exacting demands Real Madrid will place on him.

“Bernardo will rise to the challenge.”

Santos finished with another glowing endorsement of his national compatriot.

“I am. I think the Madrid fans – who are very knowledgeable and have seen the world’s best footballers pass through their stadium – will love Bernardo Silva.

“I don’t think he’ll let anyone down and that he’ll more than live up to the expectations placed on him.

“I’m sure he’s highly motivated because he loves a challenge, and this one is a massive one. He’s a magnificent player, of the highest calibre. And he’ll prove it in white,” he concluded.

title: Supporters to vote on new club crest - what do you think?
description: Newcastle say eligible supporters will be able to vote on new club crest options in August as work continues to update the existing badge.  Eligible supporters will consist of men's and women's season ticket holders, Mags and Mags+ members as of 18 July 2026 and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's matches during the 25-26 season at Gateshead International Stadium.
fullText:
Newcastle say eligible supporters will be able to vote on new club crest options in August as work continues to update the existing badge.

The club announced they were undergoing a "thoughtful evolution" process to update their crest, moving away from the design they have used since 1988, in May 2025.

Following meetings with a variety of audiences at supporter events and focus groups, the club said there was a clear message to "simplify and protect what already exists".

"Do not reinvent and do not start again," the club added. "Craft an elegant, flexible, modern visual identity, but only if the club's core heritage is protected."

Eligible supporters will consist of men's and women's season ticket holders, Mags and Mags+ members as of 18 July 2026 and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's matches during the 25-26 season at Gateshead International Stadium.

What do you make of the situation? Are you glad supporters are being allowed to have their say on the future crest, or do you still think it shouldn't even be being changed?

title: Brazil's Lula extends lead over Flavio Bolsonaro ahead of presidential vote, poll shows
description: (Changes headline in July 15 story to reflect Lula extending lead over Flavio Bolsonaro) SAO PAULO, July 15 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has widened his lead over right-
fullText:
(Changes headline in July 15 story to reflect Lula extending lead over Flavio Bolsonaro)

SAO ‌PAULO, July 15 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio ‌Lula da Silva has widened his lead over right-wing Senator ​Flavio Bolsonaro in the run-up to October's presidential election, a Quaest poll commissioned by brokerage Genial showed on Wednesday.

It is the first Quaest survey following a ‌series of political ⁠developments including a corruption investigation targeting a senior Lula ally and arguments about ⁠accountability over new U.S. tariff threats against Brazil.

• Leftist Lula is seen with 45% of voting intentions ​in a ​potential second-round runoff, compared ​with 37% for Senator ‌Bolsonaro, currently the main opposition hopeful.

• A Quaest poll in June showed Lula leading Flavio Bolsonaro by 44% to 38%.

• In a first-round scenario, Lula would take 40%, Bolsonaro 28%, Ronaldo Caiado 4%, Renan ‌Santos 3% and Romeu Zema ​2%.

• In Brazil, if no ​candidate gets more ​than 50% of valid votes, the ‌two frontrunners go to a ​second-round vote.

• ​Quaest surveyed 2,004 people between July 10 and 13. The poll has a margin of error ​of two ‌percentage points in either direction.

title: Nancy Mace reveals Senate decision as Lindsey Graham's sister redraws the race
description: Darline Graham told Sean Hannity "I'm in" after Trump pushed her to seek the Republican nomination in South Carolina's crowded Senate special election.
fullText:
As the field for the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham takes shape, one prominent lawmaker is declining to dive into the arena hours after the contest was shaken up.

Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., opted against a bid after she strongly suggested that she'd throw her hat into the arena on Monday in a video statement. Last August, Mace launched a campaign for the Republican gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Henry McMaster, but she finished a disappointing fifth out of five major candidates in last month's Republican primary.

"I want to be straight with you, I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now," Mace said. 

"I'm not chasing another title, I'm chasing justice, justice for me, justice for other women and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent," she continued. "That's where my focus is today, and that's where it's going to stay."

Fox News Channel's 'Hannity' To Air Exclusive First Interview With Sen Darline Graham On Monday

Her decision followed a move by Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., to take up her brother's mantle after she was appointed to finish off the remainder of her late brother's term. She made her decision to officially enter the upcoming special election after a push from President Donald Trump.

Graham, during an interview with Fox News' Sean Hannity, acknowledged the "tremendous amount of pressure" to take over the position and run.

"I feel like I can do it, I feel an inner peace about it," Graham said. "Will it be difficult? Yes."

When asked if she had made a decision on what she would do, she told Hannity she had.

Her addition to the growing roster of hopefuls to replace the late lawmaker will likely shake up the race less than one day before candidates can begin officially filing to run for the nomination.

Graham will come up against other challengers in her bid to claim the GOP nomination. Reps. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., Russell Fry, R-S.C., and businessman Mark Lynch, have all publicly committed to running.

And as the filing deadline fast approaches, both Mace and Norman turned their steel against their colleague, Fry, in what's shaping up to be a messy, Palmetto State brawl.

Mace argued that should Fry win the nomination, and subsequently the general election, he would vacate his "Republican House seat until next May."

Republicans Defy Trump, Jump Into South Carolina Senate Race Despite His Handpicked Successor

"That narrows an already razor-thin majority and puts the Speaker's gavel in play," Mace said. "I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift."

"If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not."

Fry announced Monday morning that he would enter the race, hours after Norman tossed in his hat, too. Mace has been making public overtures that she wants to jump in, but has so far not made anything official.

A source in Fry's political orbit told Fox News Digital that the lawmaker has "accomplished more than all the potential candidates combined," having passed three bills in his first four years in Congress.

"This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation," they said.

Still, the conservative duo are going for blood against Fry, arguing that his bid for the seat is purely politically motivated and jeopardizes the House's already slim majority heading into the midterm elections later this year.

Under South Carolina law, Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot. He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina's 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he'll be on the ballot twice for both.

Trump Reveals Who He's Eyeing To Replace Lindsey Graham

Ultimately, he'll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place.

The top two Republicans in the South Carolina Statehouse, South Carolina Senate President Thomas Alexander and South Carolina House Speaker Murrell Smith, said in a statement that the upcoming special election has "highlighted issues in South Carolina law," and noted that, "In the coming weeks, we intend to address these important issues so that we can provide the process available for our citizens."

That is not a problem for either Mace nor Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month.

"SC Republican voters soundly rejected Nancy and Ralph as recently as last month because conservatives are tired of career politicians who care more about their own social media than getting actual results for families," South Carolina state Sen. JD Chaplin, and a spokesperson for Fry's campaign, told Fox News Digital. 

Norman was less venomous in his take on the situation, and noted on X that he respected Fry and had enjoyed his time serving with him in the House. Still, his candidacy, he argued, "could leave his seat vacant until May 18th."

"This would leave his constituents unrepresented, jeopardize President Trump's agenda, and put the House at risk of being led by a Speaker Hakeem Jeffries," Norman said. "We can't let that happen!!"

Senate Majority Leader Sen. John Thune, speaking to reporters on Monday afternoon, referred to Darlene Graham and said, "She obviously carries on Lindsey's legacy. But I think she's a very accomplished person in her own right."

Asked if he would be endorsing her, the longtime lawmaker from South Dakota and top Republican in the Senate said, "I'm not endorsing anyone at the moment."

Fox News' Tyler Olson contributed to this report

Original article

title: Vote for Gibsonburg Golden Bears athlete you remember first since 2000
description: Which Gibsonburg Golden Bears athlete since 2000 stands out the most to you? Vote now to crown the most memorable.
fullText:
Too many athletes have played a varsity sport at Gibsonburg since 2000 to remember them all.

Some, of course, were more memorable than others. Who do you remember first for the Golden Bears in the last 26 years?

Vote for your choice from 3 p.m. Monday to 3 p.m. Friday.

High school basketball girls Katy Hasselbach didn't feel pain in name of SJCC girls basketball

Sarah Taulker, who graduated in 2002, was first-team in the Suburban Lakes League for volleyball, basketball and softball as a senior. She had 379 assists in one season for volleyball. She finished her basketball career with 1,077 points. She was twice a state champ for softball.

Mallory Fleming, who graduated in 2004, was first-team in the Suburban Lakes League for volleyball and basketball as a junior and senior. She qualified to state in the high jump as a sophomore and helped a 4x200 relay finish sixth in Ohio as a senior. She continued her volleyball career at Findlay.

Gage Beaber, who graduated in 2012, was player of the year in the Toledo Area Athletic Conference for basketball as a senior. He was honorable mention all-state for baseball, swiping 45 bases. He was first-team in the Suburban Lakes League for baseball as a sophomore and first-team for basketball as a junior. He continued his basketball career at Trine University and Heidelberg.

Brad Mendoza, who graduated in 2019, finished third twice and fifth once at the state wrestling tourney. He was first-team in the Toledo Area Athletic Conference as a sophomore and junior and first-team in the Sandusky Bay Conference River Division as a senior for football. He qualified to state on a 4x100 relay. He earned 13 letters, including baseball. He continued his wrestling career at Ohio University.

Jamie Wonderly, who graduated in 2003, was twice first-team all-state for softball. She was player of the year in the Suburban Lakes League three times. She was a state champ for softball three times. She won 95 games, which ranks sixth all-time in the state. She collected 1,264 strikeouts, sixth all-time, 12 no-hitters and three perfect games. She also played volleyball for four years. She continued her softball career at Butler.

Connor Smith, who graduated in 2023, is fifth all-time in the state with 3,433 yards rushing for a season as a senior. He had 48 touchdowns, including one reception and 47 scores on the ground, which is ninth all-time in Ohio for a season. He rushed for 98 career touchdowns. He finished with 6,929 yards on the ground. He rushed for 921 yards and 10 touchdowns in three postseason games as a senior. He won a state wrestling championship as a junior and finished fourth as a sophomore. He ran track as a senior. He continued his football career and switched to wrestling at Findlay.

Krissy Lotycz, who graduated in 2002, was player of the year for volleyball and first-team for softball in the Suburban Lakes League as a senior. She was twice a state champ for softball. She also played basketball. She continued her softball career at Tiffin.

Jordan Kreglow, who graduated in 2015, was player of the year in the Toledo Area Athletic Conference as a senior for basketball. He surpassed 1,000 points for his career. He was first-team all-league for baseball as a senior. He continued his basketball career at Trine University.

Heather (Hill) Hall, who graduated in 2001, scored 22 points in the fourth quarter in a sectional final basketball victory. She finished her career with 1,463 points, including 40 in one game. She was player of the year in the Suburban Lake League and first-team all-state as a senior. She was first-team all-league for softball as a junior. She won a state crown as a senior. She was first-team all-conference three times and established a career volleyball record for assists. She was a captain on all but one of six teams her final two seasons that went a combined 127-22. She continued her basketball career at Tiffin.

Jenna Bloomfield, who graduated in 2023, finished her softball career with 33 home runs, including 16 in one season. She registered 807 strikeouts in her career, including 295 as a senior. She twice was first-team all-state. She also played basketball and volleyball. She continued her softball career at Toledo.

This article originally appeared on Fremont News-Messenger: Vote for Gibsonburg Golden Bears athlete you remember first since 2000

title: Vote for Woodmore Wildcats athlete you remember first since 2000
description: Here are 10 of the most memorable Woodmore Wildcats athletes since 2000. Which one do you think was the best? Vote now!
fullText:
Too many athletes have played a varsity sport at Woodmore since 2000 to remember them all.

Some, of course, were more memorable than others. Who do you remember first for the Wildcats in the last 26 years?

Vote for your choice from 3 p.m. Monday to 3 p.m. Friday.

High school basketball boys Jake Meisler changed, helped maintain boys basketball at Margaretta

Evan Ulinski, who graduated in 2014, is the only wrestler in school history to qualify for the state tournament four times. He placed second twice and fourth once. He established program records with 184 wins for his career and 51 in a season. He notched 117 pins in his career, including 37 in one season. He won four league crowns in the Northern Buckeye Conference. He was honorable mention all-state as a receiver for football as a senior. He caught 63 passes. He also played baseball. He continued his wrestling career at Air Force.

Emily Pendleton, who graduated in 2007, was the first Ohio athlete to win the same event four times at the state track tourney. She won four state crowns in the discus. She was Gatorade national athlete of the year for girls track as a senior. She holds the state record for any division at 183-3 for the discus. She was second in the shot put at state as a senior and fourth as a junior. She also played volleyball. She continued her career at Michigan.

Catie Lavigne, who graduated in 2002, was first-team in the Suburban Lakes League for basketball as a senior. She dropped in the game-winning bucket to advance to regional. She had 19 assists in one game. She qualified to the state track meet four years, including a second-place finish on the 4x400 relay as a junior.

Olivia Vogelpohl, who graduated in 2024, helped a 4x400 relay finish second and a 4x800 finish fifth at state as part of an overall team championship. She also ran cross country.

Maddie Phillips, who graduated in 2014, was pitcher of year in the Northern Buckeye Conference as a senior and sophomore. She was first-team all-state as a senior. She tossed four perfect games. She also played volleyball. She continued her softball career at Urbana.

Tyler Rosenberger, who graduated in 2005, was Suburban Lakes League player of the year for basketball as a senior and junior. He was first-team all-league as a sophomore. He finished with 1,309 points and 227 steals. He was first-team all-conference at quarterback for football as a senior and part of the program record 4x400 relay for track. He continued his basketball career at Ashland.

Brooke Allen, who graduated in 2021, established program records in basketball, volleyball and softball. She shared player of the year honor in the Northern Buckeye Conference for basketball as a senior. She finished with a program high 1,209 points. She has the most kills and aces in a career for volleyball. She hit the most home runs in a season and career for softball. She continued her basketball career at Ohio Northern.

Azure Travis, who graduated in 2024, won consecutive state championships in both hurdle events. She helped a 4x400 relay finish second at state as part of an Ohio team championship as a junior. She holds the program record in all three events. She was seventh in the 100 and 200 as a senior. She also played soccer. She continued her track career at Marshall.

Landon Rich, who graduated in 2026, finished his career with program records for rushing yards (4,788) and touchdowns (53) on the ground. He set single-season marks with 1,993 yards and 32 touchdowns rushing as a senior. He established single-game records with 465 yards and seven touchdowns, on 35 carries against Northwood. He qualified for state for wrestling at 165 pounds. He was part of a fifth-place 4x100 relay at state as a senior and qualified in the 100 as a junior. He continued his football career at Baldwin-Wallace.

Ava Beam, who graduated in 2021, was athlete of the year in the Northern Buckeye Conference for track and finished third at state in the 3,200. She qualified to state three years, with the pandemic eliminating any chance as a junior. She also played basketball. She continued her track career at Findlay.

title: Who had Martin Dentistry best softball hitting performance of 2026?
description: Vote for San Joaquin County best single-game softball hitting performance of 2025-26 as Martin Dentistry AOTW series looks back on the school year.
fullText:
The school year may be over, but the debates are just beginning.

With another high school sports season complete, The Record is shifting the Martin Dentistry Athlete of the Week series into offseason mode. We'll spend the summer revisiting the biggest performances, moments and accomplishments from across 2025-26.

Last week, readers weighed in on the best single-game hitting performances from the baseball season. Now it's time for the girls to take center stage.

We've selected 15 of the most impressive single-game softball hitting performances from the 2025-26 season. Now it's up to you to decide which one deserves the top spot.

The poll closes at noon Thursday, July 23. There are no voting restrictions, so vote now and vote often.

Scroll below the poll for a closer look at the 15 performances up for consideration.

Coffman's six RBIs, home run not enough as Mountain House falls on opening day

The final score didn't take away from what Mackenzie Coffman did on opening day. The Mountain House senior did everything she could to keep her team in it, going 3-for-4 with a home run and six RBIs in a 15-12 loss to Kimball on Feb. 12.

Wilson shows veteran approach as freshman, walks four times in Edison debut

First high school games usually come with nerves. Jamaiya Wilson looked like she had been there before. The Edison freshman reached base four times on walks in her debut against Grant on Feb. 19 and added an RBI double, the first of her 34 RBIs that season.

Edison's Tatupu drives in seven, homers in 18-run rout of Sonora

Lelia Tatupu spent the entire season bringing runners home. Against Sonora on Feb. 28, the Edison senior put together her biggest performance, going 4-for-5 with two triples, a home run and seven RBIs in a 24-6 victory.

Tracy's Cargill rises to occasion with six-RBI performance against California top-40 team

Big moments require big performances. Emalee Cargill delivered hers against one of California's best. The Tracy senior and Cal signee went 3-for-3 with a home run and six RBIs in a 13-3 victory over Central Catholic on March 5, which finished No. 39 in California according to MaxPreps.

Martinez proves she's more than a pitcher with five RBIs, home run for Ripon

Most knew what Angelina Martinez could do as a pitcher. On March 7, she reminded everyone what she could do at the plate. The Ripon senior went 2-for-3 with a home run and five RBIs in the first game of a back-to-back against Livingston.

Sansaver pulls off rare offensive feat, hits for cycle as Tracy beats Lodi

A single. A double. A triple. A home run. Taylor Sansaver checked every box on March 19. The Tracy junior and University of Arizona signee completed the rare cycle, finishing 4-for-4 with five RBIs in a 12-4 win over Lodi on March 19.

Shanks drives in five without needing extra-base hit for Bear Creek

Not every big offensive performance requires extra-base hits. Shayla Shanks proved that against Stagg on April 2. The Bear Creek freshman drove in five runs while going 3-for-6, with all three hits being singles, in a 22-0 victory.

BHCPA's 26-run performance comes behind Drulias' cycle against ALHA

Julieann Drulias spent the season driving in runs at a county-leading pace, and April 8 showed exactly why. The BHCPA senior and Jackson State signee went 6-for-6 with five RBIs, recording a single, double, triple and home run in a 26-5 victory over ALHA.

River Island's Orndoff puts up video game numbers with 13 RBIs, three home runs

Some stat lines don't look real at first glance. Octavia Orndoff's performance on April 29 was one of them. The River Island junior went 5-for-5 with three home runs, a double, a triple and 13 RBIs, finishing just a single short of the cycle in a 21-0 win over ALHA.

Linden's Schallberger career eight-RBI day at plate highlights wild 34-run game

Thirty-four combined runs. Plenty of offense to go around. Yet Katie Schallberger still managed to steal the spotlight. The Linden senior went 3-for-6 with a home run and eight RBIs in a 22-12 win over Riverbank on April 30.

Calderon ends VOL play with six RBIs, two home runs for Manteca

Third place in the VOL standings was already set, but Na'ima Calderon made sure Manteca ended league play with momentum. The junior went 3-for-4 with two home runs and six RBIs in a 12-4 victory over Mountain House on May 4.

Sansaver drives in six as Tracy stuns Kimball for third time in TCAL play

Kimball entered the season as the team expected to push Tracy in the TCAL. Instead, Taylor Sansaver helped close the door on that possibility. The Tracy junior went 3-for-5 with six RBIs in a 13-0 victory on May 6, as the Bulldogs finished off their third mercy-rule shutout of Kimball.

Granata's big day helps Liberty Ranch move on in CIF SJS playoffs

The postseason can bring pressure. Bella Granata brought production. The Liberty Ranch senior and San Diego State signee opened the CIF Sac-Joaquin Section Division V playoffs by going 4-for-4 with a home run and six RBIs, leading No. 4 Liberty Ranch past No. 13 Woodland Christian 18-0 on May 13.

Fears stays hot at plate as Linden advances past Bear River in CIF SJS quarterfinals

The postseason lights got brighter. Olivia Fears only got hotter. Less than 48 hours after a perfect 4-for-4 performance, the Linden senior and University of the Pacific signee delivered again, going 5-for-5 with a home run, a triple and four RBIs to lead No. 3 Linden past No. 6 Bear River 14-8 in the CIF Sac-Joaquin Section Division VI quarterfinals on May 15.

Babasa turns years of losing into triumph as Escalon captures CIF SJS title No. 6

Sixteen wins and 47 losses didn't define Madison Babasa's Escalon career. What came next did. The University of Arizona signee delivered when Escalon needed it most, going 4-for-4 with two doubles, a triple and three RBIs as No. 7 Escalon upset No. 5 Casa Roble in the CIF Sac-Joaquin Section Division V championship on May 23, securing the program's sixth Section title.

This article originally appeared on The Record: Who had Martin Dentistry best softball hitting performance of 2026?

title: Who Is the U.K.’s New Prime Minister?
description: New U.K. Prime Minister Andy Burnham rose through the ranks of the Labour party to become Prime Minister without winning a single General Election. So how did he do it?
fullText: New U.K. Prime Minister Andy Burnham rose through the ranks of the Labour party to become Prime Minister without winning a single General Election. So how did he do it?

title: Sen. Warner says he won't vote to confirm Jay Clayton as DNI
description: But with Republicans controlling the Senate, he expects Clayton to be confirmed.
fullText:
Democratic Sen. Mark Warner, the vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said Sunday that he will not vote for Jay Clayton, currently the U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York, to be confirmed as director of national intelligence, a key intelligence oversight role.

"I've known Jay for a long time. I like him, but after his performance before my committee, his inability to answer the question about who won the 2020 election ... I am not going to vote for him," Warner told ABC News' "This Week" co-anchor Jonathan Karl.

Warner's statement comes after Clayton was grilled by Democrats on the Senate Intelligence Committee last week, some repeatedly asking him who won the 2020 presidential election. Clayton refused to say that former President Joe Biden was the winner, saying only that he was "certified" as the winner.

Still, Warner said he expected Clayton to be confirmed.

'I'm not an election denier': Clayton, Trump's DNI pick, faces tense questions on 2020 election

"I think Jay Clayton will get confirmed," Warner said. "And God willing, I hope the guy that becomes DNI is the guy that I've known and liked and worked with."

Clayton can be confirmed without the support of Democrats as Republicans control the Senate.

President Donald Trump last week delivered a primetime address to the nation in which he accused some foreign countries, specifically China, of attempting to interfere in American elections starting in 2020, an accusation which China denied.

In the speech, Trump announced the declassification of several documents he claimed showed that China had pulled off "the largest compromise of election data in history" by acquiring information on American voters. Voter file data is broadly available, either for free or for purchase, from any state in the U.S.

Key takeaways from Trump's elections speech

Senators on the Intelligence Committee from both parties, including Warner, have said that the newly released documents reflected mostly previously known information about attempts to interfere in the 2020 election and showed no proof that the election was stolen, as Trump has frequently said.

Warner said Sunday that while China is a concern, he sees Russia and Iran as bigger threats.

"Russia was very good at influencing our elections. They have been far and away the most prominent player, usually on behalf of Trump. Iran has been very active as well, usually against Trump," Warner said. "China's got the capabilities, but they've been ... less about which candidate and more like, can we just make Americans lose confidence?"

A Russian spokesman said Friday that it has never interfered in U.S. elections.

As Trump continues to falsely claim that the 2020 election was stolen and that U.S. elections are not secure, the president again pushed for the passage of the SAVE America Act, a sweeping voting bill that would require photo ID to vote and proof of citizenship, such as a passport or a birth certificate, to register to vote.

Democrats are united against the bill, which does not currently have enough support in the Senate to become law. But asked Sunday if he would support a narrower requirement to require photo ID in U.S. elections, Warner said he would support a uniform ID requirement, but that the SAVE America Act goes too far.

"If you're going to have a set of IDs, let's have it across the board as long as it comes from some official point," Warner said. "But this is not about voter ID. The SAVE Act is about voter disenfranchisement."

title: Vote for the Register male Athlete of the Week for July 12-18
description: Here are the Register's male Athlete of the Week candidates from the metro area for the week of July 12-18.
fullText:
The week of July 12-18 saw the IHSAA state baseball tournament field set for all four classes.

Eight teams from the Des Moines metro area qualified for the state baseball tournament. Indianola, Waukee, Johnston, West Des Moines Valley and Southeast Polk qualified in Class 4A; Carlisle and North Polk advanced in 3A and Ankeny Christian is competing in 1A.

There were several standout individual performances from area baseball players during substate competition.

Here are the Des Moines Register's male Athlete of the Week candidates from the Des Moines metro area for the week of July 12-18. DesMoinesRegister.com readers vote to determine the winners, who will be announced at the end of the week.

Mincks threw a strong complete game for the No. 6 Johnston baseball team during its 2-1 victory over Dowling Catholic in the 4A Substate 3 championship game on July 15 at Johnston.

Mincks surrendered one run on four hits and one walk in seven innings to earn the win and help his team advance to state. He finished the game with four strikeouts.

Berg had an explosive hitting night for No. 2 Carlisle during its 14-5 victory over Harlan in the 3A Substate 8 championship game on July 15 at Carlisle.

Berg went 3-for-4 with one double and two solo home runs. He also was 3-for-4 with a solo home run during a 9-8 substate semifinal win over Winterset in eight innings on July 13 at Carlisle.

Sanders helped Southeast Polk upset No. 5 Cedar Rapids Kennedy in an 11-inning marathon during the 4A Substate 5 championship game on July 15 at Cedar Rapids.

Sanders doubled once, picked up one sacrifice hit and drove in three runs for the Rams.

Blake was brilliant on the mound for No. 3 Indianola during its 8-0 victory over No. 8 Waukee Northwest in the 4A Substate 8 championship game on July 15 at Indianola.

Blake fired a complete-game two-hit shutout for the Indians. He struck out nine batters and did not issue a walk.

Fincham helped Ankeny Christian upset No. 3 Saint Ansgar in a 12-10 slugfest during the 1A Substate 3 championship game on July 14 at Mason City.

Fincham went 3-for-5 at the plate with a triple. He scored three runs and tallied two steals and RBIs apiece.

Joe Randleman covers high school sports for the Ames Tribune. Contact him at jrandleman@gannett.com. Follow him on Twitter at @JoeRandleman

title: Vote for the Register female Athlete of the Week for July 12-18
description: Here are the Register's female Athlete of the Week candidates from the metro area for the week of July 12-18.
fullText:
The week of July 12-18 saw the IGHSAU state softball tournament field set for all five classes.

The Des Moines metro area saw a total of 11 teams qualify for the state softball tournament.

Defending champion Waukee Northwest, Ankeny Centennial, West Des Moines Valley, Dallas Center-Grimes, Southeast Polk and Dowling Catholic are all competing in Class 5A. The 4A field includes Adel-Desoto-Minburn, Carlisle and North Polk; Two-time defending 2A champion Van Meter is now competing for a 3A state title and Grand View Christian will be playing in 2A.

There were several outstanding individual performances from area softball players during regional final competition last week.

Here are the Des Moines Register's female Athlete of the Week candidates from the Des Moines metro area for the week of July 12-18. DesMoinesRegister.com readers vote to determine the winners, who will be announced at the end of the week.

Fetters had a big night swinging the bat for the 5A No. 4 DCG softball team during its 11-3 victory over No. 15 Cedar Rapids Prairie in the 5A Region 4 championship game on July 14 at Grimes.

Fetters went 2-for-3 with a home run. She both scored and drove in two runs apiece to help the Mustangs advance to state.

Thamontri keyed No. 6 Southeast Polk's 5-0 victory over Ottumwa in the 5A Region 6 championship game on July 14 at Pleasant Hill.

The Ram catcher went a perfect 3-for-3 at the plate with one double. She scored and drove in one run apiece and also added one steal.

Lucas helped the No. 8 North Polk softball team down Boone, 5-1, in the 4A Region 6 championship game on July 14 at Alleman.

Lucas was 1-for-3 at the plate, and she drove in three runs for the Comets. She also made two putouts and had no errors in the field.

Karaidos helped No. 6 Van Meter pound No. 8 Atlantic by a 9-1 score in the 3A Region 5 championship game on July 14 at Van Meter.

The Bulldog third baseman was 3-for-4 with a double and home run. She finished the game with two runs and three RBIs.

Cox helped No. 2 Grand View Christian roll past No. 14 West Marshall by a 12-4 score in the 2A Region 1 champion game on July 13 in Des Moines.

Cox was the winning pitcher after allowing two earned runs on eight hits with six strikeouts and one walk in five innings. She also homered and drove in three runs for the Thunder.

Joe Randleman covers high school sports for the Ames Tribune. Contact him at jrandleman@gannett.com. Follow him on Twitter at @JoeRandleman
