title: Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards
description: BBC Sport reporters pick their best moments of the tournament - and you can vote for yours.
fullText:
After delivering unforgettable drama and surprises across 104 matches over almost six weeks, the World Cup is over.

History has been scripted, records broken and heroes old and new made their mark at football's most coveted tournament.

Read our reporters' reflections from the US, Canada and Mexico and then choose from our chief football writer Phil McNulty's selections for best goal and best save.

Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.

Mbappe first to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player

Lamine Yamal 'completes football' at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

World Cup is over, but the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Vote for the best Vermont high school sports dynasty of the last 50 years powered by Delta Dental
description: It's time to vote for Vermont's best high school sports dynasty of the last 50 years powered by Delta Dental.
fullText:
From yearslong championship runs and sheer dominance to impressive consecutive winning streaks, dynastic programs are weaved into high school sports history in Vermont. And over the last 50 years, this state has seen its share of tradition-rich programs rack up wins and titles, from a diverse offering of sports.

But which dynasty is the greatest? To answer that question, Burlington Free Press reporters Alex Abrami and Judith Altneu plucked out 20 deserving nominations of the last five decades. Now it's your turn, the reader, to cast your vote in the latest BFP summer ballot powered by Delta Dental.

For a starting point in this subjective process, the Free Press looked at teams with at least five years (or more) of extended greatness.

Read on and then vote away! Print readers can find the poll at burlingtonfreepress.com.

After back-to-back penalty shootout losses, the former in one of the state's great upsets, the Champlain Valley boys soccer program reeled off six consecutive Division I state titles (counting a tie in 2003 with BFA-St. Albans). Led by Dan Shepardson for the first four and his former player T.J. Mead for the last two, the Redhawks went 94-5-5 over their championship run.

Since 2011, the CVU girls soccer team has captured 11 D-I crowns and finished runner-up twice with seven unbeaten seasons. Stan Williams, who stepped down after the 2024 season, has helmed the majority of the run, with eight titles and a 182-9-6 record over a dozen years that includes a 61-game winning streak from 2017-2020.

Since 2003, the CVU girls cross-country running team has raced to 21 out of 23 D-I titles, including 12 in a row from 2009-2020 under former coach Scott Bliss. But Redhawks are also a regional powerhouse, with nine New England crowns (2003, 2010, 2011, 2016, 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024, 2025) and five appearances at nationals (2010, 2011, 2018, 2024, 2025).

Since volleyball became a varsity sport in 2016, Essex girls volleyball has appeared in every championship game winning seven of the nine possible titles. Jen Liguori leads a team that has posted five straight undefeated seasons with an 86-game winning streak dating back to 2021.

Hartford field hockey dominated Division II winning four consecutive titles from 1994-1997 (one tie) with current head coach Heather Scudder at the helm. The Hurricanes moved up to Division I in 1998 and proceeded to advance to seven straight championship games from 1999-2005, winning six straight titles (includes one tie in in 2002).

Between 2007-2012, Hartford football stormed to five D-I titles, including three straight from 2010-12. The Hurricanes are the lone D-I team to seize a three-peat and they owned a 27-game winning streak that stretched over four seasons. In two of their perfect seasons, the Hurricanes tallied 915 points over 22 games while rushing for 4,056 yards in 2012.

Under coach Mike Gonneville, Northfield girls soccer ripped off a 69-game winning streak, the nation's third longest at the time, and seven straight titles in D-III between 1995-2001. The Marauders also had a 74-game unbeaten streak snapped in 2000. Over the seven championship seasons, Northfield went 114-8-2. After losing in the semis in 2002, Northfield then proceeded to three-peat.

Stowe boys soccer seized seven straight D-III championships between 2012-2018. Then the Raiders jumped to D-II and captured the 2019 title for eight in a row and an unbeaten season at 17-0-1. Brian Buczek led Stowe to six of those crowns and went 86-11-8 in his tenure.

In the early days of hockey, it's no surprise BFA-St. Albans was the team to beat. The Bobwhites claimed seven D-I championships in a row between 1973-79, with a pair of unbeaten seasons that includes 18-0 in 1975 when they blasted Spaulding 7-3 in the final. During the seven-year run, BFA went 114-11-2.

BFA-St. Albans has been the state's most consistent girls hockey program since the Vermont Principals' Association added the sport staring with the the 2001-2002 season. The Comets lead the state with 13 girls hockey championships and won the first five D-I titles (2002-2006). Longtime head coach Luke Cioffi has guided BFA to appearances in 19 of the state's 25 championship games.

When CVU girls basketball won in 2013, the Redhawks ended a 26-year title drought. More titles and more wins kept flowing: Under coach Ute Otley's direction, CVU seized five straight D-I crowns and compiled a 96-game winning streak that set a Vermont all-time modern record. In four out of the five seasons, a Redhawk was named the Free Press' Miss Basketball including Emily Kinneston in back-to-back years (2013, 2014).

Essex girls basketball dominated the sport in the late 1990s, posting back-to-back undefeated seasons in 1995 and 1996 and accumulated a 63-game winning streak. Libby Smith was the star for the Hornets during their five-year consecutive championship run. The Hornets would add their third undefeated season in four seasons in 1998 before losing in the 1999 and 2000 championship games.

Essex dominated gymnastics, winning every championship meet over a dozen years between 2006-2017. The Hornets also had every all-around state champion over their run, paced by Mary Savage (Krug), who seized four straight wins from 2006-09.

Mount Anthony boys basketball had one of Vermont's most dominant runs in the sport capturing five straight Division I championships. During that five-year run, head coach Dave Fredrickson compiled a 102-13 record. To earn that last title in 1992, Mount Anthony rallied late in the fourth quarter and pulled away in overtime for a 56-51 win over Colchester.

Mount Anthony wrestling had its remarkable championship run earlier this year. But the Patriots have truly been kings of their sport in Vermont. They seized 36 straight state titles, a national record for wrestling programs and the sixth most out of all sports according to NFHS. The Patriots, led by national coach of the year honoree Scott Legacy for the majority of their reign, also feasted on their competition out of state, racking up eight New England crowns and seven runner-up finishes since 1989.

During Essex's girls track and field's historic run, three different coaches — Steve Dowd, Karen Albright and Joe Gonillo — led the program, but the standard remained consistent. In Gonillo's first season as the girls coach in 1997, Essex set a then-national record for the sport, capturing its 13th straight Division I title. During that 13-year stretch the Hornets claimed 38 individual titles and set five state records.

Champlain Valley boys lacrosse have been a tough out come postseason time no matter its seed. The Redhawks have won 12 of the last 13 Division I lacrosse titles, including a record-setting nine in a row. The only year CVU did not win came in 2023 when they got bounced in the semifinals. Three of the Redhawks titles came when they were seeded fourth or lower including their 2025 title.

For six seasons in the mid-2000s, Lyndon softball dominated the sport winning 79-straight games from 2005-2009 and four straight D-II titles. Three of those state championships were decided in one-run games. Sandwiched between that four-peat, Lyndon suffered losses in the 2004 and 2009 D-II title games.

The South Burlington boys tennis team went on a tear in the late 1980s-1990s with record-setting coach Steve LaTulippe who won over 200 matches during his tenure. South Burlington captured seven consecutive titles with five total undefeated seasons during its dynasty. South Burlington lead the state with 25 total boys tennis championships.

After finishing as the runner-ups in 1995, South Burlington girls tennis used that loss as motivation fueling an extended run where the team advanced to 12 straight championship matches, winning nine total titles under longtime coach Jake Agna, who retired prior to the 2023 season. That run also covers a five-peat from 1999-2003, during which Sara Hughes claimed four straight individual titles.

Contact Alex Abrami at aabrami@freepressmedia.com. Follow him on X, formerly known as Twitter: @aabrami5.

Contact Judith Altneu at JAltneu@usatodayco.com. Follow her on X, formerly known as Twitter: @Judith_Altneu.

title: Vote for who the greatest forgotten hero is in Indiana basketball history
description: It's now your turn to determine who the greatest forgotten hero in Indiana high school basketball is. Voting is unlimited through Thursday, July 23.
fullText:
On July 8, we presented our list of the top forgotten heroes from Indiana high school basketball history as part of a special "America 250" project by Indiana high school sports journalists within USA Today Company.

Now, it's your turn to vote for who you think is the best of the best.

title: Fetterman on Trump election speech: ‘Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020’
description: Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Sunday said President Trump could “do something positive” instead of talking about the 2020 presidential election, clapping at the president’s primetime speech on Thursd...
fullText:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Sunday said President Trump could "do something positive" instead of talking about the 2020 presidential election, clapping at the president's primetime speech on Thursday about election security.

"I don't know why the president is dragging up all this stuff," Fetterman told CNN's Jake Tapper on "State of the Union." "You're the president. Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020. That's been settled over six years ago. It's deeply unhelpful and that distracts important things, especially like the Iranian war right now."

On Thursday, Trump said recently declassified documents suggest China interfered in the 2020 presidential election by acquiring more than 200 million U.S. voter files. A 2021 report from the intelligence community about foreign threats to the 2020 elections concluded with "high confidence" that China did not interfere in the election but did take steps to try to undermine Trump's reelection.

Fetterman, who was Pennsylvania's lieutenant governor at the time, said the 2020 presidential election was "absolutely secure, fair and accurate."

"That was the voice of Pennsylvania, and they picked Joe Biden in 2020, that's a fact," Fetterman said. "… Now, until I see evidence that over 25 million migrants that … voted or registered to vote, then yeah, that would make me reconsider some of these things. But there's no evidence, and I don't expect that we'll see any of that."

The Pennsylvania Democrat questioned "why the president is dragging up all of this stuff."

Trump used his primetime speech, amid the backdrop of his ongoing war with Iran, to accuse the intelligence community of failing to sound the alarm as China penetrated U.S. voter rolls. Another confusing claim was that the intelligence community "worked to actively suppress and downplay information about the extent of China's sinister election meddling, covering it up from both the president and the American people."

Trump used the speech to suggest that there should be changes to U.S. elections, including the passage of his legislative priority requiring proof of citizenship to register to vote and photo ID to cast a ballot.

"Bogus" was how Sen. Mark Warner (Va.), the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, described Trump's claims.

"The fact is our intelligence agencies unanimously agreed that China did not even try to change a single vote in the 2020 election," Warner wrote on social platform X. "A single concurring opinion suggested China may have tried to sway voters' opinions… but that's been public knowledge since 2021."

Some Republican lawmakers criticized the speech before and after it aired. Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) said on Friday after the speech that the problem was not that the elections were "stolen," adding that Republicans "won the damn elections."

"White House, Senate, House, even the Supreme Court leans our way," Massie wrote on X. "The fraud is that our party got elected and broke our campaign promises to voters."

title: ‘Pulte got really scared’: Inside the White House debate over Trump’s elections speech
description: Acting Director of National Intelligence Bill Pulte, a Trump favorite, had at one point pushed to reveal the names of intelligence officials accused of concealing election interference from the presid...
fullText:
President Donald Trump's Thursday night speech was far tamer than some of his closest aides feared.

Acting Director of National Intelligence Bill Pulte, a favorite of the president's, had in the days leading up the speech pushed to reveal the names of intelligence officials accused of concealing election interference from Trump, according to two senior officials granted anonymity to discuss private conversations.

That set off a scramble inside the White House with senior officials, including chief of staff Susie Wiles, fearing that it could undermine the president's broader point about election vulnerabilities and put intelligence analysts' lives at risk, the two officials said.

They argued to Pulte that naming individuals involved in an alleged cover up of election fraud could lead to conspiracy theorists to take the law into their own hands.

Pulte, who flew to New York with Trump on Friday, did not respond to a request for comment but the two officials said he backed down.

"Pulte got really scared by the whole thing and pulled back," said one of the two senior officials. "He got POTUS all spun up and then was incredibly scared when he realized people could die with his reckless behavior."

Simultaneously, several Trump officials urged the president to remain restrained and on script, a notoriously difficult task for the president, whose aides often learn what the president is going to say as he says it.

"They explained the way to be taken seriously is not to be crazy," the senior official told POLITICO.

The combination of a full-court press from some aides and Pulte's retreat convinced the president to stay "on the rails" as the senior official put it.

"President Trump and millions of Americans agree that we must have integrity in our elections, otherwise we have no country at all," said Davis Ingle, a White House spokesperson. "Congress should immediately pass the SAVE America Act."

The acting director and former housing official, who took over for Tulsi Gabbard this month, has a history of bombastic ideas that don't always work out as the administration might hope. He made headlines last year for recommending the Department of Justice investigate Democratic lawmakers, Federal Reserve board member Lisa Cook and New York Attorney General Letitia James over unproven allegations of mortgage fraud.

None of the allegations have led to a conviction.

Pulte also advocated for Congress to investigate then-Fed Chair Jerome Powell over the renovation of the central bank's headquarters and promoted a widely panned 50-year mortgage idea that he got Trump to champion.

Trump's decision to allow him to temporarily oversee the intelligence community was met with bipartisan criticism from senators who felt he was underqualified.

Less than two weeks later, the White House nominated Jay Clayton, the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York. Clayton is expected to be confirmed soon.

Trump on Thursday night declassified documents that he claimed demonstrated startling vulnerabilities in the U.S. election system. He described efforts by China to access U.S. voter rolls and offered details about long-studied risks with electronic voting machines.

The documents, some of which remain heavily redacted, tread on well-worn ground and reflect a genuine split among intelligence analysts who debated and ultimately concluded that China opted against a large-scale influence effort during the 2020 election cycle.

Pulte, according to the two White House officials, had wanted fewer redactions.

"There was going to be a lot of crazier shit said," the White House official said.

Even Trump's closest aides, however, don't necessarily believe the story is over. Trump remains animated by election fraud and considers passing the SAVE America Act a top priority. Despite his Thursday speech, the legislation is unlikely to gain any new traction in Congress.

"POTUS was happy with the way the speech played," the second official said. "But I don't know if he'll keep pushing this or if this was enough to get it out of his system."

title: As Trump accuses China of stealing voter data, Xi pitches Beijing as a responsible tech leader
description: As US President Donald Trump accused Beijing of exploiting US election data in a televised speech in Washington, halfway across the world in China, Xi Jinping was sending a very different message.
fullText:
As US President Donald Trump accused Beijing of exploiting US election data in a televised speech in Washington, halfway across the world in China, Xi Jinping was delivering a very different message.

Beijing is a responsible global leader bent on shaping the future of technology for good, Xi intoned to hundreds of tech executives, researchers and industry figures gathering in Shanghai Friday for the opening of China's flagship artificial intelligence summit.

"With AI advancing at a staggering speed, we must ensure its development is for positive, for good, and for humanity," Xi said in an opening address to the conference. "We must make its oversight and governance precise and effective and constantly refine measures to forestall loss of control."

Xi spoke minutes after Trump laid out a litany of claims against the Chinese government, including that it had illicitly acquired 220 million American voter files amid broader efforts to influence US elections. China has denied the allegations.

The juxtaposition of the two messages illuminates the deepening faultlines and anxieties within the technological competition between the US and China, which the rapid rise of AI is only deepening.

Xi's message – a clear bid for China to helm the setting of global rules around AI – comes at a moment of fierce US-China competition over the technology, as well as intense concern about its national security implications, including AI's ability to exploit software and database vulnerabilities.

In his address, Xi hit back against "overstretching the national security concept in the field of AI" or "placing one country's security over that of others" – veiled allusions to how Beijing sees the American approach to the technology.

Instead, China has looked to push forward a message that the technology should be a "global public good" – and that it is willing to work with countries to develop it together.

On the eve of the conference, China launched its World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization (WAICO), a new grouping of 29 countries, including Russia, Indonesia and Pakistan, friendly to China and its aims.

"Xi sees AI as an opportunity to get more allies to compete with the US, not just in AI technology, but also in international relations – (this is) AI diplomacy," said George Chen, the Hong Kong-based chair of digital practice at The Asia Group consultancy.

China feels it missed the chance to set the rules on the global development of the world wide web over recent decades, he added, but the arrival of AI finds it in a much stronger position.

"Thirty or forty years ago, China was a very poor country … but everybody knows today is different, and if AI is the new internet, China doesn't want to miss the opportunity again."

US companies are widely seen to be racing to the frontier of the technology as their core strategy to win the competition. Their models still largely hold the lead in capabilities, as well as the hardware used to train and advance them.

But that gap is narrowing. And when it comes to winning the AI race, Beijing is banking on a different tack: applying and scaling up AI technology in robotics and automation – as well as large-scale adoption globally, experts say.

Chinese artificial intelligence firms like DeepSeek and Zhipu have made major leaps toward closing the performance gap with US firms.

An increasing number of users around the world are also opting for their models' open-source format and lower operating costs relative to Silicon Valley's offerings.

Chinese firms accounted for 20 of the daily top 50 AI models on OpenRouter in May, a platform which allows users to interact with a wide variety of models, up from only five at the start of 2025, according to an analysis by Our World In Data. Most others are American.

Washington in recent months alleged that Chinese entities were engaging in "deliberate, industrial-scale campaigns to distill US frontier AI," referring to a process by which a smaller model trains off a larger one to improve its own capabilities.

Earlier this month, a Chinese regulator warned it had identified ‌a serious security "backdoor" risk in US firm Anthropic's Claude Code tool. Anthropic said the so-called backdoor was an experimental mechanism to track abuse of its platform and that access to it was not allowed in China.

There are also deep-seated concerns in Washington that foreign actors could use powerful AI models to find and exploit cybersecurity vulnerabilities in US critical infrastructure. The White House earlier this week launched an effort to address those.

Beijing is also exploring potentially restricting overseas access to China's most advanced AI models, Reuters reported earlier this month, citing sources.

Both countries agreed to start an AI dialogue following a May summit between Trump and Xi in Beijing.

The strengthening foothold of Chinese AI models globally may help China's ambitions to lead the technology's proliferation and regulation.

The conference in Shanghai shows both the extent and the limits of its reach, with limited involvement of American firms despite what state media called record attendance at this year's event.

Attendees of the four-day Shanghai conference include UN Secretary General António Guterres, nine Nobel laureates and Turing computing prize awardees, as well as more than 1,000 global enterprises, organizers said.

This is the first time Xi has attended the flagship event since its launch in 2018, a clear signal of the importance Beijing attaches to AI and the mounting competition with the US to lead its future.

Western analysts have raised concerns that Beijing's expanding role setting global norms around AI will enable it to export the norms of its own highly restrictive media and internet environment.

And there are questions around how much global interest Beijing can generate in its new WAICO international body.

No major Western country will likely sign on to a China-controlled organization that will likely have a broad mandate for both the promotion of AI and AI governance and safety, according to Paul Triolo, a partner at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group consultancy in Washington.

"For the US, the main action will be building a credible bilateral dialogue with Beijing around frontier AI model governance," he said.

"Both sides must deal with complex bureaucratic challenges around the issue, and deep distrust on both sides."

title: Reactions to Trump's election integrity speech, are well, divisive
description: Despie declassifying documents and providing no evidence prior U.S. election returns had been jeopardized, reactions to Trump's speech were naturally divisive.
fullText:
Reaction was swift, varied, and mostly along party lines following President Donald Trump's July 16 primetime address announcing a litany of declassified documents to sow doubt on the integrity of U.S. elections, fewer than four months from the pivotal midterm elections.

Trump said U.S. enemies, including Russia, China, Iran and North Korea, could compromise the nation's election infrastructure and that a so-called "deep state" within the government concealed intelligence showing China's interference during the 2018 midterm and 2020 presidential elections during his first term.

The president used his list of election allegations, which could not be immediately verified, to urge Republicans in Congress to pass the Senate-stalled SAVE America Act, which would significantly overhaul voting in federal elections.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, D-New York, said in a series of social media posts that the "SAVE Act is dead on arrival" in the Senate and that Trump's speech was a "pathetic attempt" to deny he lost the 2020 election.

"This president sadly wallows in grievance, vengeance, and conspiracy while Americans want their leaders to boldly tackle persistent inflation, expensive groceries, rising gas prices, and terrible cuts to affordable healthcare," Schumer said, adding that Democrats will not let Trump use "delusional bias" to undermine Americans' right to vote.

Meanwhile,  Department of Homeland Security head Markwayne Mullin backed the president's sentiments, noting that his agency has identified more than 250,000 potential noncitizens illegally registered to vote in just four U.S. states.

"Election security is national security," Mullin said in a post on X. "Only Americans should be electing American leaders."

'Shocking vulnerabilities'?: Trump overstates election weaknesses in speech: 5 takeaways

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, in a post on X, called Trump "pathetic" and "a feeble, unhinged conspiracy-peddling 80-year-old failed President.

"The economy is a disaster under this guy and the American people know it," the Democratic caucus leader concluded.

Sen. Mark Warner, D-Virginia, vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said he was embarrassed for the nation, calling Trump's speech an attempt to undermine the nation's election integrity.

"I'm embarrassed that the President of the United States tried to speak to the whole nation with a whole series of falsehoods, accusations, I believe, aimed at trying to undermine confidence in our system," Warner said, telling MS NOW talk show host Jen Psaki if America has a "free and fair election in 2026," he and his allies will lose the midterm election in dramatic fashion.

"If we simply blow this off as another Donald Trump rant, we do that at our own peril," Warner said.

Warner said that in the past 10 years, his committee, in a bipartisan manner, has reviewed and investigated all of the allegations the president brought up.

"The irony of all of this is most of the accusations he's making about 2018 and 2020, Donald Trump's appointees were in charge of the intelligence community," Warner said. "Why didn't they find any of these so-called allegations?"

Warner urged his Republican colleagues, if they have any dignity, to speak up and "tell the truth."

But Georgia Rep. Mike Collins, who's also the state's Republican Senate candidate and is endorsed by Trump, said on social media Americans should be "alarmed" by their enemies' attempts to prevent secure elections, echoing the president's comments.

"Our right to free and secure elections should be protected relentlessly, and every American should be alarmed by our enemies' ability to disrupt our elections and jeopardize our right to vote," Collins said in a post on X. "Eighty-three percent of Americans support voter ID, and there is no time more important than now to pass the SAVE America Act and safeguard democracy."

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, in an X post of his own, seemed to agree. "Thank you, @POTUS, for standing up for free and fair elections. Americans deserve confidence in the integrity of our elections, and that is vital for our country."

Trump focuses on an election: But, it's not the midterms in November. It's 2020

However, Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse D-Rhode Island, said Trump's seemed to be the "ceremonial kickoff" campaign to interfere in the crucial midterm elections come November.

Whitehouse said Trump is losing independent voters, two years after the president fared well with the group in his reelection victory.

"Even MAGA is disheartened," Whitehouse said in a written statement. "(Trump) is failing and unpopular, and he's dragging his party down with him in the midterms."

The lawmaker said Trump was trying to use the powers of his office to "taint an American election, backed by his coterie of creepy billionaires."

Whitehouse said his colleagues "will be ready" to defend Americans' right to vote, citing they have systems to protect the vote and lawyers to defend them.

"The important thing right now is for the public to see and understand what this lawless and increasingly unhinged president is trying to do," Whitehouse said.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Reactions to Trump's election integrity speech, are well, divisive

title: Trump says these documents prove his false claims of election fraud. Here's what they really say
description: President Donald Trump released a trove of documents during a primetime address to the nation that allies had hyped as a smoking gun that would prove his long-debunked allegations of mass voter fraud....
fullText:
WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump released a trove of documents during a primetime address to the nation that allies had hyped as a smoking gun that would prove his long-debunked allegations of mass voter fraud.

Speaking from the White House on Thursday night, he described shocking revelations, like Chinese meddling to undermine his failed candidacy in 2020 and a cover-up by the "deep state." He claimed, "Americans were blatantly lied to about the security of our election infrastructure."

But a review by The Associated Press found no such confirmation in the collection of newly declassified reports, investigation files, intelligence analysis and assorted correspondence. Many pages are so heavily redacted that their findings are unclear. Others outline vulnerabilities and assessments that have been well-documented for years. There's no evidence that China or any other foreign entity manipulated the vote in 2020 or any other year.

"The White House promised a bombshell, and they delivered a dud," said David Becker, executive director of the Center for Election Innovation & Research, who attended a White House briefing on the material ahead of the speech. Despite what appeared to be a concerted effort by administration officials, "there was absolutely nothing here that was news, nothing here that even calls into question past elections and certainly not the 2020 election."

Here's a look at what the documents say.

"Starting during the 2020 election cycle, the People's Republic of China carried out what is believed to be the largest compromise of election data in history, resulting in China's illicit acquisition of 220 million U.S. voter files," Trump said Thursday night.

There is no evidence, however, that China actually used that information in any way.

It has long been established that China collects immense volumes of data on Americans unrelated to any efforts to manipulate votes or alter election outcomes. And public versions of voter files are widely available, including online, and can be bought and sold by campaigns and political parties so they know which doors to knock on and where to send mail.

China's efforts to influence the 2020 campaign were already well-documented, and there was no assessment of any direct election interference. The records released Thursday night do not refute that conclusion, but reveal an internal intelligence community debate about how to characterize Beijing's efforts and motives.

The emails show that a dissenting viewpoint was that China had taken steps to "denigrate" Trump. But that perspective, instead of being hidden, was already reflected in the intelligence community assessment produced after the election.

China on Friday called Trump's allegations "groundless" and "entirely fabricated" and said it has never interfered in U.S. elections and has no interest in doing so.

In his remarks, Trump touted the release of a new Department of Homeland Security investigation, based on state voter rolls and public records, that he said had identified approximately 278,000 noncitizens registered to vote in federal elections.

The report said the agency uncovered more than a quarter of a million noncitizens illegally registered to vote in California, Pennsylvania, New Jersey and Nevada, based on public records, which are often erroneous or incomplete. Another 28,000 noncitizens, it claimed, were found on voter rolls in 25 states using the new Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements system.

There is no allegation, however, that any of those people actually voted, which would be a crime.

That data has also not been verified. Reports have found that the SAVE database is plagued with errors, including outdated information that has often classified naturalized citizens as noncitizens. Indeed, a federal judge has barred the database from being used over fears that voters will be wrongly purged from voter rolls.

Studies have found noncitizen voting to be extremely rare. Noncitizens are also allowed to vote in some local elections, and could be on the rolls for that reason.

Trump has spent years criticizing the intelligence community's assessment that Russia meddled in the 2016 election to help him win. But the documents shed new light on the country's ongoing efforts.

One declassified document from 2020 portrays Russia as the country that has tried the most to penetrate American election systems — but in an effort to defeat Joe Biden. It notes how Russia worked to amplify claims that Biden, while serving as vice president, engaged in inappropriate behavior involving Burisma, the Ukrainian energy firm that employed his son Hunter, a charge frequently repeated by Trump and Republicans.

"Their aim is to defeat the former Vice President and ensure the President's victory," reads the document from the National Intelligence Council.

The document went on to state that both China and Iran wanted Trump to lose. But it included a chart documenting each country's known efforts. Only Russia was marked as having been known to have engaged in "targeting, accessing, or manipulating election processes or election-related systems."

Russia has continued to deny interfering in U.S. affairs.

Many of the documents released involve a Michigan case in which a seemingly pro-Biden canvassing operation submitted thousands of questionable voter registrations to a local election official in 2020. The official did not accept the registrations and alerted authorities.

The documents include notes from at least one FBI agent that are heavily redacted but indicate that the agent unsuccessfully pushed for further investigation and charges through 2024. Michigan Republicans in 2023 complained the state's Democratic attorney general did not charge anyone.

However, the case was closed "because logical investigation and/or leads have been exhausted, and the investigation to date did not identify a criminal violation or a priority threat to national security," according to one of the records.

The documents, Trump told the nation, included intelligence "revealing shocking vulnerabilities in our election infrastructure" that leave it vulnerable "to hacking, exploitation and foreign interference."

One report included a list of recent breaches -- mostly by Russia — and called on state and local election officials to step up their defenses to prevent information from being used to obtain absentee ballots or alter voter rolls. Election officials acknowledge that voting machines also carry risk -- which is why they don't rely on them alone to ensure the accuracy of the vote. Safeguards like physical security, equipment testing, paper ballot backups and post-election audits help to catch machine errors or threats.

But it is unclear what the administration is doing to facilitate their efforts. Earlier this month, Trump ousted members of a bipartisan federal election commission that distributes federal grants to states, oversees the testing of voting systems and maintains the national voter registration form, after the group resisted his efforts to require potential voters to document their U.S. citizenship before registering.

Trump has also cut millions of dollars in federal funding from the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, known as CISA, which helped state and local election officials keep elections secure, and disbanded an FBI task force focused on investigating foreign influence operations, including those that target U.S. elections.

The documents make repeated reference to the risks posed by large databases of voter information, including registration databases and election websites that could be accessed or manipulated by foreign adversaries.

But the SAVE system, which Trump has been pushing states to adopt, has been criticized as an unlawful, centralized federal database of voter information, which could be another target.

Colvin reported from New York. Associated Press writers Nicholas Riccardi, Ali Swenson, Katie Vogel and Mary Clare Jalonick contributed to this report.
